background: Consider a sequence of 25 Bernoulli trials (binomial situation) where each trial results in either success or failure. From the 25 trials, 6 failures and 19 successes result. Let p be the probability of success, and 1-p the probability of failure.

task.query: Find the estimator of p, \hat{p}, which maximizes that particular outcome.
task.answer: The sample density function can be written as $$ g(19)=\binom{25}{19} p^{19}(1-p)^6 $$ The maximum of $$ g(19) $$ occurs when $$ p=\hat{p}=\frac{19}{25} $$ so that $$ g\left(19 \left\lvert\, p=\frac{19}{25}\right.\right) \geq g\left(19 \left\lvert\, p \neq \frac{19}{25}\right.\right) $$ Now $$ \mathrm{g}(19) $$ is the probability or 'likelihood' of 6 failures in a sequence of 25 trials. Select $$ p=\hat{p}=\frac{19}{25} $$ as the probability or likelihood maximum value and, hence, $$ \hat{p} $$ is referred to as the maximum likelihood estimate.

task.query: Discuss the method of Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE).
task.answer: The principle of maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), originally developed by R. A. Fisher in the 1920s, states that the desired probability distribution is the one that makes the observed data 'most likely,' which means that one must seek the value of the parameter vector that maximizes the likelihood function. The mechanics for obtaining the MLE can be summarized as follows: Step 1. Find the joint density function $$ L(X, \theta) $$. Step 2. Take the natural $$ \log $$ of the joint density, $$ \ln L(\theta) $$. Step 3. Take the partial derivatives of $$ \ln L(\theta) $$ (or $$ L(\theta) $$) with respect to each parameter. Step 4. Set partial derivatives to 'zero.' Step 5. Solve for parameter(s).
